stem: A hospital's diagnostic pathway for a rare condition involves three tests administered sequentially. Test 1 (screening) has sensitivity 92% and specificity 85%. Only patients who test positive on Test 1 proceed to Test 2 (confirmatory), which has sensitivity 88-94% and specificity 95%. Only patients positive on Test 2 proceed to Test 3 (definitive), which has sensitivity 98% and specificity 99%. The disease prevalence in the screened population is 2%, but there is a referral bias: patients referred to this hospital are 2-4 times more likely to have the disease than the general screened population, so the effective prevalence for this hospital is between 4% and 8%. A patient tests positive on all three tests. What is the probability they have the disease? Also compute: if the patient tested negative on Test 3 (but positive on Tests 1 and 2), what is the probability they have the disease?

ground_truth.answer: P(disease | all 3 positive): [0.936, 0.987] depending on prevalence and Test 2 sensitivity. Point estimate: 0.968. P(disease | pos-pos-neg): [0.027, 0.108]. Point estimate: 0.058.

solution_steps: Sequential Bayesian updating: use posterior from each test as prior for the next.
Varying prevalence [4%, 8%] and Se2 [0.88, 0.94] gives final posterior range [0.936, 0.987].
For T3 negative case: P(D|T3-) = (1-Se3)×P(D after T2) / [(1-Se3)×P(D after T2) + Sp3×P(¬D after T2)].
Varying parameters: P(D|pos-pos-neg) ∈ [0.027, 0.108], showing that a negative definitive test substantially reduces but does not eliminate disease probability.
common_failure_modes: Not performing sequential updates — using prevalence as the prior for all three tests simultaneously
Ignoring the referral bias adjustment to prevalence
Not recognizing that the sequential positive results dramatically increase the effective prior for each subsequent test
For the negative Test 3 case: concluding the patient definitely doesn't have the disease
Not jointly varying the prevalence and Test 2 sensitivity uncertainties
Computing the wrong conditional direction (P(test result | disease) instead of P(disease | test result))
